passages.explanation: In Iconium, Paul and Barnabas go to the Jewish synagogue and speak so effectively that a great number of Jews and Greeks believe. But the Jews who refuse to believe stir up the Gentiles and poison their minds against the brothers. Paul and Barnabas stay a considerable time, speaking boldly, and the Lord confirms their message with signs and wonders. The city is divided — some side with the Jews, some with the apostles. When a plot forms among both Gentiles and Jews to mistreat and stone them, they find out and flee to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe. They keep preaching. The pattern from chapter 13 continues — boldness, fruit, opposition, movement.

In Lystra, Paul heals a man who has been lame from birth — he's never walked. Paul sees that the man has faith to be healed and calls out: stand up on your feet! The man jumps up and walks. The crowd's reaction is unexpected: they start shouting in Lycaonian that the gods have come down in human form. They call Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, because Paul is the chief speaker. The priest of the temple of Zeus brings bulls and wreaths to the city gates to offer sacrifices to them. The locals have a legend that Zeus and Hermes once visited their region in disguise (told in Ovid's Metamorphoses), so they're determined not to miss the gods again.

When Paul and Barnabas realise what's happening, they tear their clothes and rush into the crowd: friends, why are you doing this? We're human beings just like you! We're bringing you good news — turn from these worthless things to the living God who made heaven, earth, sea, and everything in them. In past generations God let all nations go their own way, yet he hasn't left himself without testimony: he gives rain and crops, food and gladness. Even with these words, they can barely stop the crowd from sacrificing to them. Paul's speech here is tailored to a pagan audience with no knowledge of Jewish scripture — no Abraham, no Moses, just creation and providence. He meets people where they are.

Jews arrive from Antioch and Iconium and win the crowd over. They stone Paul and drag him outside the city, thinking he's dead. But the disciples gather around him, he gets up, and goes back into the city. The next day he and Barnabas leave for Derbe. The reversal is staggering — the same crowd that tried to worship Paul as a god now stones him. Public opinion is volatile, and popularity is not the same as faith.

In Derbe they preach the gospel and win a large number of disciples. Then they do something remarkable: they retrace their steps through Lystra, Iconium, and Pisidian Antioch — cities where they were persecuted — to strengthen the disciples. They encourage them to remain true to the faith, warning that we must go through many hardships to enter the kingdom of God. They appoint elders in each church, pray and fast, and commit them to the Lord. They don't just plant churches and leave — they build leadership structures and prepare people for suffering.

Paul and Barnabas pass through Pisidia into Pamphylia, preach in Perga, go down to Attalia, and sail back to Antioch — where they'd been commissioned for this work. They gather the church and report everything God has done through them, especially how he opened a door of faith to the Gentiles. They stay in Antioch a long time. The first missionary journey is complete. It started with the Holy Spirit's call in 13:2 and ends with a church report. The model for missionary work is established: sent by the church, accountable to the church.
